coredata.dc:title: Amphipathic carbon quantum dots-functionalized silica stationary phase for reversed phase/hydrophilic interaction chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Qi
Hou, Xiudan
Zhang, Xiangfei
Li, Hui
Zhao, Liang
Lv, Haitao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are considered as good chromatographic separation materials. However, due to the hydrophily of the synthesized CQDs, their applications in HPLC are limited to HILIC for separating strong polar compounds only. In this work, a novel amphipathic CQDs with both hydrophobicity and hydrophily is developed as mixed-mode stationary phase for RPLC/HILIC. To give CQDs certain hydrophobicity, 1,8-diaminooctane is chosen as one of the carbon sources for introducing alkyl chain into CQDs. The amphipathic CQDs modified silica (CQDs/SiO2) stationary phase has typical characteristic of RPLC/HILIC. Both hydrophobic and hydrophilic compounds including alkylbenzenes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, nucleosides and bases, amino acids, β-adrenoceptor blockers and agonists, sulfonamides, antibiotics and alkaloids obtain satisfactory separation on this CQDs/SiO2 column. 14 nucleosides and bases commonly existing in living organisms achieve good separation on this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column within 25 min and the resolutions reach 1.33–13.83 with an average column efficiency of 18,800. The retention mechanism of this novel CQDs/SiO2 column is investigated by linear solvation energy relationship model. It is found that hydrophobic interaction, π-π stacking, hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic interactions are main retention interactions under RPLC mode. This work provides a new approach for synthesis of amphipathic CQDs. Also, it indicates that amphipathic CQDs with versatile functional properties have great prospect in separation science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Stationary phases
Reversed phase liquid chromatography
Hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography
Retention mechanism
Linear solvation energy relationship

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CQDs and CQDs/SiO2 composite

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31901766 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB087 ), the Talents of High Level Scientific Research Foundation , Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 6651120016 ), and the Support Plan on Science and Technology for Youth Innovation of Universities in Shandong Province (No. 2019KJM002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic process of CQDs bonded silica stationary phase.
TEM images of (a) amphipathic CQDs, (b,c) amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 and (d) Cl/SiO2.
LSCM images of (a) Cl/SiO2 and (b) amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 under 405 nm excitation.
FTIR image of the prepared amphipathic CQDs and CQDs/SiO2 composite.
Plots of log k versus the number of methylene groups of alkylbenzenes under the condition of mobile phase containing 20% acetonitrile and 30% methanol.

Chromatograms for the separation of PAHs on (a) amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column, (b) citric acid CQDs column and (c) commercial C18 column. Analytes: (1) naphthalene, (2) fluorene, (3) anthracene, (4) pyrene, (5) benz(a)anthracene; Mobile phase: (a) 60% MeOH/H2O, column pressure: 60 bar; (b) 60% MeOH/H2O, column pressure: 65 bar; (c) 90% MeOH/H2O, column pressure: 20 bar; flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1; The concentration of analytes in the injected sample was 50–100 μmol/L, the inject volume was 5 μL.

Chromatograms for the separation of sulfonamides on (a) amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column, (b) citric acid CQDs column and (c) commercial C18 column. Analytes: (1) sulfadiazine, (2) sulfamerazine, (3) sulfamethazine, (4) sulfapyridine, (5) sulfathiazole; Mobile phase: (a) 10% MeOH/20 mM ammonium acetate, column pressure: 87 bar; (b) 30% MeOH/20 mM ammonium acetate, column pressure: 75 bar; (c) 20% MeOH/20 mM ammonium acetate, column pressure: 45 bar; flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1; The concentration of analytes in the injected sample was 50–100 μmol/L, the inject volume was 5 μL.
Plot of log k versus volume fraction of ACN in mobile phase.

Chromatograms for the separation of nucleosides and bases on (a) amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column, (b) citric acid CQDs column and (c) commercial NH2 column. Analytes: (1) thymine, (2) uracil, (3) thymidine, (4) uridine, (5) adenosine, (6) cytosine, (7) adenine, (8) cytidine, (9) inosine, (10) 6-hydroxypurine, (11) guanine, (12) guanosine, (13) 2,6-dihydroxypurine, (14) xanthosine; Mobile phase: (a) 0–5 min 95% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate, 5–15 min 90% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate, 15–25 min 80% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate; column pressure: 31–40 bar; (b) 0–15 min 85% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate, 15–20 min 80% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate, 20–30 min 60% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate; column pressure: 35–50 bar; (c) 0–4 min 90% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate, 4–15 min 85% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate, 15–20 min 80% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate, 20–35 min 50% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate; column pressure: 30–64 bar; flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1; The concentration of analytes in the injected sample was 50–100 μmol/L, the inject volume was 5 μL.
Experimental log k versus calculated log k plot for CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase.
Specific surface area, pore volume and pore size data.
System coefficients and statistics of CQDs/SiO2 column.

head.ce:title: Amphipathic carbon quantum dots-functionalized silica stationary phase for reversed phase/hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. No.18, Tianshui Middle Road, Lanzhou, China.
Corresponding author. No.700, Changcheng Road, Chengyang District, Qingdao, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are considered as good chromatographic separation materials. However, due to the hydrophily of the synthesized CQDs, their applications in HPLC are limited to HILIC for separating strong polar compounds only. In this work, a novel amphipathic CQDs with both hydrophobicity and hydrophily is developed as mixed-mode stationary phase for RPLC/HILIC. To give CQDs certain hydrophobicity, 1,8-diaminooctane is chosen as one of the carbon sources for introducing alkyl chain into CQDs. The amphipathic CQDs modified silica (CQDs/SiO2) stationary phase has typical characteristic of RPLC/HILIC. Both hydrophobic and hydrophilic compounds including alkylbenzenes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, nucleosides and bases, amino acids, β-adrenoceptor blockers and agonists, sulfonamides, antibiotics and alkaloids obtain satisfactory separation on this CQDs/SiO2 column. 14 nucleosides and bases commonly existing in living organisms achieve good separation on this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column within 25 min and the resolutions reach 1.33–13.83 with an average column efficiency of 18,800. The retention mechanism of this novel CQDs/SiO2 column is investigated by linear solvation energy relationship model. It is found that hydrophobic interaction, π-π stacking, hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic interactions are main retention interactions under RPLC mode. This work provides a new approach for synthesis of amphipathic CQDs. Also, it indicates that amphipathic CQDs with versatile functional properties have great prospect in separation science.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanoparticles or nanodots (CDs), have received great attention in many research fields such as bioimaging and sensing, drug delivery, optoelectronic devices due to their outstanding merits including good chemical stability, excellent photoluminescence stability, good dispersibility, low toxicity and biocompatibility [1,2]. With the wide applications of CQDs in analytical chemistry, they also aroused research interest of scientists devoted in separation science [3–5]. An important research direction in separation science is solid-phase extraction which allows extracting the required analytes from the complex matrices for easy and sensitive analysis, especially as a sample pretreatment technology before chromatographic analysis [6–8]. CQDs have been more and more applied in solid-phase extraction in recent years. Mashkani and coworkers prepared magnetically N-doped CQDs as a sorbent for the preconcentration and extraction of trace amounts of Pb2+from water and vegetable samples [9]. Owing to the high surface area of CQDs, satisfactory preconcentration results were achieved by using fewer amounts of the sorbent. Dong and coworkers prepared MoS2/CDs composite for dispersive solid-phase extraction of brominated flame retardants in water [10]. Benefiting from good dispersing capability of MoS2 and hydrogen bond interaction between CDs and the target analytes, the MoS2/CDs sorbent exhibited good extraction performance. Chen and coworkers designed two hydrophilic molecularly imprinted nanospheres for solid-phase extraction by using CDs as a kind of hydrophilic and environmentally friendly core [11,12]. In the aspect of CQDs applied as chromatographic separation materials, Qiu's group synthesized silanized CDs [13], tryptophan-based CDs [14], polyethyleneimine-based CDs [15], imidazolium ionic liquids-derived CDs [16], phenylenediamine-based CDs [17], glucose and nitrogen-doping glucose-based CDs [18,19] used as stationary phases for hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC), many kinds of hydrophilic compounds such as saccharides, ginsenosides, nucleosides and bases obtained well separation on these CDs-decorated silica columns.

It can be speculated that CQDs are a kind of good separation materials. First, CQDs synthesized by two or more carbon sources possess stable carbon core and abundant functional groups on their surface, they can be considered as a kind of multi-functional separation material with large specific surface area and good mechanical property [20]. Second, by choosing different carbon sources, CQDs with different functional properties can be synthesized to meet different separation requirements [21]. Third, CQDs are smaller than other carbon nanomaterials such as graphene and carbon nanotubes, with high thermal stability and excellent solubility, they can be easier modified on micron-sized porous silica supports [22]. Last but not least, one-step modification of CQDs with rich functional groups on silica supports avoids traditional multi-step modification of silica by organic molecules, and simplifies the preparation process of chromatography stationary phases [15].

The separation of various kinds of compounds on a single column is the goal of chromatography analysts. The research and development of mixed-mode stationary phases which are suitable for separation of several kinds of compounds including hydrophobic, polar and hydrophilic compounds is always an important research topic in separation science [23–25]. RPLC/HILIC mixed-mode chromatography has obtained great attention in the field of chromatography since they have strong separation capacity for all kinds of compounds and good applicability in the analysis of complex samples especially in the case of liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry.

We think CQDs should be a good candidate for mixed-mode chromatography stationary phases since their versatile functional properties including hydrophilic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding properties. However, the current applications of CQDs in HPLC are all in HILIC according to our literature research. This is due to that most of the synthesized CQDs are hydrophilic, thus their applications in chromatography are limited to separation of strong polar substances by HILIC. To the best of our knowledge, there have been no example of CQDs applicated in RPLC for separation of hydrophobic compounds, let alone in mixed-mode chromatography. We wonder can we prepare amphipathic CQDs with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties, so as to enable them to be used as mixed-mode stationary phase for RPLC/HILIC.

According to literature research, using carbon sources containing hydrophobic groups during the synthesis process of CQDs can transfer the hydrophobic properties to the synthesized CQDs [26,27]. Inspired by this, 1,8-diaminooctane is chosen as one of the carbon sources for introducing alkyl chain into CQDs, citric acid which is commonly used for synthesizing CQDs is chosen as another carbon source for the synthesis of hydrophilic/hydrophobic CQDs in this work. The reason for choosing 1,8-diaminooctane as one of the carbon sources for synthesizing amphipathic CQDs is that chromatography stationary phases with long alkyl chains usually show excellent RPLC separation performance [28–30]. We hope that the synthesized CQDs containing alkyl chains will have certain hydrophobic property and RPLC separation performance in addition to the inherent hydrophilic property of CQDs. The synthesized CQDs were modified onto silica and used as mixed-mode stationary phase for RPLC/HILIC. This amphipathic CQDs-decorated silica stationary phase showed good selectivity for hydrophobic and aromatic compounds under RPLC mode, and satisfied separation performance for hydrophilic compounds under HILIC mode. To get an in-depth understanding of the retention mechanism, linear solvation energy relationship (LSER) model was established for the CQDs/SiO2 column to obtain magnitude estimate of individual interaction.

Silica gel (300 m2/g, 100 Å, 5 μm in diameter) was supplied by Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd (Japan). 3-Chloropropyltrimethoxysilane, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, 1,8-diaminooctane, citric acid, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Meryer Chemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used for chromatographic separation were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Organic reagents (HPLC grade) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2100 F field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra was acquired on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) images were obtained on a FLUOVIEW FV1200 biological confocal laser scanning microscope (OLYMPUS, Japan). Surface area and pore size analysis was carried on NOVA 2200e surface analysis instrument (Quantachrome, USA). Contact angle test was performed on KRÜSS DSA25 standard contact angle measuring instrument (KRÜSS, Germany). Thermogravimetric analysis was carried on METTLER TOLEDO TGA2 thermal gravimetric analyzer (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland).

Amphipathic CQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. Citric acid and 1,8-diaminooctane were used as carbon sources. In a typical synthesis procedure, 2.0 g citric acid and 1.0 g 1,8-diaminooctane were dissolved uniformly with 20 mL deionized water, then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 3 days and the final solid product was recovered by freeze drying. To explore the effect of introducing 1,8-diaminooctane as carbon source on the chromatographic performance of CQDs-based stationary phases, stationary phase based on CQDs prepared by using pure citric acid (citric acid CQDs/SiO2) was also fabricated in this work. Citric acid CQDs were prepared using the same method as amphipathic CQDs’ except that citric acid was used as the sole carbon source.

The synthetic process of amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 composite is shown in Fig. 1 . First, 3.0 g silica gels were suspended in 75.0 mL dry toluene and then 1.0 mL 3-chloropropyltrimethoxysilane was added into above suspension. The suspension was stirred at 100 °C for 24 h to receive chloropropylated silicas (Cl/SiO2). After cooling to room temperature, the Cl/SiO2 composites were washed with toluene, ethanol, methanol and dried at 60 °C for 12 h under vacuum. Second, 2.5 g Cl/SiO2 composites were dispersed in 75 mL methanol, then 0.2 g of the prepared CQDs and 1.0 mL triethylamine were added into above dispersion, the dispersion was stirred at 80 °C for 16 h. After the reaction, CQDs/SiO2 composite was washed with methanol, deionized water and then dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

Citric acid CQDs-based stationary phase was prepared as follows. First, aminopropyl silica gels were prepared by using the method of preparing chloropropylated silicas except that 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane was used to modify silica. Second, 0.2 g citric acid CQDs were dispersed into 75 mL deionized water, 0.29 g EDC (1.5 mmol) and 0.17 g NHS (1.5 mmol) were added into the above CQDs aqueous dispersion and the mixture was stirred for 30 min to activate the carboxyl groups of CQDs. Then, 2.5 g aminopropyl silica were added to the mixture and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. After the reaction, citric acid CQDs modified silica were washed with water and methanol by filtration for several times and then were dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

The CQDs/SiO2 composites (about 2.0 g) were packed into stainless steel tubes (150 mm × 4.6 mm i. d.) by a slurry packing method. The packing pressure was 40 MPa with methanol and isopropanol (1/1, v/v) as homogenate solvents and methanol as propulsion solvent.

All chromatographic separation was performed on a Thermo UltiMate 3000 HPLC system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), consisted of an UltiMate 3000 pump, an UltiMate 3000 autosampler and an UltiMate 3000 diode array detector. The UV detection wavelength was set at 254 nm. Nonaromatic amino acids were detected by precolumn derivatization with o-phthalaldehyde which was commonly used for detection of amino acids by HPLC [31]. Commercial Purospher STAR RP-18 endcapped Hibar RT (5 μm, 150 mm × 4.6 mm i. d.) column was used as a comparison in RPLC mode. Commercial Purospher STAR NH2 LiChroCART (5 μm, 150 mm × 4.6 mm i. d.) column was used as a comparison in HILIC mode. The preparation of two components mobile phases such as MeOH/H2O and ACN/ammonium acetate are according to their volumeratio (v/v).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and CQDs/SiO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of amphipathic CQDs and CQDs/SiO2 was characterized by TEM and LSCM. The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) shows uniform circular shaped and mono-dispersed CQDs with diameters mainly in the range of 1.5–3.5 nm and the mean size is about 2.5 nm. The HRTEM image (bottom inset of Fig. 2a) clearly displays the lattice of CQDs, which indicates that the CQDs are highly crystalline in nature. From the TEM images of amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 composite (Fig. 2b and c), it can be seen that the surface of silica gel is covered with a thin layer of CQDs, which indicates that silica gel is functionalized with CQDs. Fig. 3 shows the LSCM images of Cl/SiO2 and amphipathic CQDs/SiO2, compared with Cl/SiO2 with no fluorescence, homogeneous fluorescence is observed on the surface of CQDs/SiO2, which indicates uniform bonding reaction of CQDs on Cl/SiO2. The LSCM imaging again demonstrates the preparation of amphipathic CQDs functionalized silica gels.

The chemical composition of amphipathic CQDs and CQDs/SiO2 was characterized by FTIR. Fig. 4 a shows the FTIR spectra of the prepared CQDs. The peak at 3429 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H. The peaks at 2928/2855 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of –CH2 and the peak at 722 cm−1 indicates the number (n) of –(CH2)n is greater than or equal to 4. The FTIR characterization result demonstrates the successful introduction of alkyl chain into CQDs. The peak at 1639 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CO in CONH which origins from the reaction of citric acid and 1,8-diaminooctane. The peak at 1405 cm−1 is derived from C–O symmetric stretching vibration of COO−. The peak at 1121 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of C–O–C. It can be concluded that the prepared amphipathic CQDs possess hydroxy, amino, carboxyl and alkyl groups. Fig. 4b shows the FTIR spectra of Cl/SiO2 and CQDs/SiO2. Compared to Cl/SiO2, the enhanced peak at 1646 cm−1 (NH–CO), 2942/2861 cm−1 (-CH2) and the disappeared peak at 611 cm−1 (C–Cl) of CQDs/SiO2 indicate the covalent attachment of CQDs to Cl/SiO2.

Specific surface area (A s), pore volume and pore size data of amphipathic CQDs and CQDs/SiO2 composite are listed in Table 1 . Since the agglomeration of CQDs without solvent, the tested A s of CQDs is relatively low. However, the A s increased after modification of CQDs on Cl/SiO2, this might be due to that CQDs were bonded onto silica in their dispersed state in the solvent. In addition, the pore volume and pore size decreased after the decoration of CQDs on Cl/SiO2, which indicated that CQDs not only bonded onto the surface but also entered into the channels of silica gel during the modification process.

The amount of amphipathic CQDs loaded on silica particles was obtained by thermogravimetric analysis. Fig. S1 shows the thermogravimetric weight loss curves for Cl/SiO2 and amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 composites. Through comparison we can deduce that the amount of load amphipathic CQDs on silica particles is about 4%, which means that the content of CQDs on silica gels is about 40 mg/g.

The hydrophilicity of the amphipathic CQDs was characterized by water contact angle test. Fig. S2 shows the water contact angle images of citric acid CQDs and amphipathic CQDs. The water contact angle of citric acid CQDs is 48.99°, while that of amphipathic CQDs prepared by citric acid and 1,8-diaminooctane is 75.38°. It can be seen that introducing 1,8-diaminooctane as another carbon source indeed increases the hydrophobicity of CQDs and thus the final stationary phase.

According to these characterization data related to the morphology, chemical composition and physicochemical property of CQDs and CQDs/SiO2 composite, it can be concluded that the amphipathic CQDs and CQDs-modified silica composite are prepared as expected.

Since the goal of using compound containing alkyl chain as carbon source was to endow CQDs certain hydrophobicity, we first investigated the chromatographic performance of this CQDs/SiO2 column in RPLC mode. It has been widely known that a distinguishing feature of RPLC is that retention factor (k) of non-polar samples often decrease exponentially upon increasing the proportion of organic modifier in mobile phases [32]. This corresponds to a linear relationship between log k and the volume or weight fraction of organic solvent.
(1) log k = log k w – Sϕ
Where k w is the extrapolated k value in pure water, ϕ is the volume fraction of organic solvent, S is the slope of log k versus ϕ when fitted to a linear regression model.

To confirm the RPLC essential character of this CQDs/SiO2 column, the relationship between log k values of alkylbenzenes and the volume fraction of MeOH in mobile phases was explored. Fig S3 displays the plots of log k verses the volume fraction of MeOH at a range of 10–50%, from which we can see that the retention of alkylbenzenes decreases continuously with the increase of MeOH content in mobile phase. Moreover, log k values and the volume fraction of MeOH has a good linear relationship and fits well to Eq. (1) with correlation coefficients of 0.9995–0.9998. The experimental results indicate that this CQDs/SiO2 column has essential characteristic of RPLC.

Another characteristic of RPLC is that k values for the members of a homologous series increase exponentially with the number of homologous units [32]. Thus, the relationship between the log k values and the number of methylene groups of alkylbenzenes was also investigated. From Fig. 5 we can see that there is a good linear relationship between log k values and the number of methylene groups at different mobile phase compositions. The above experimental results indicate that this CQDs/SiO2 column has typical characteristic of RPLC and good hydrophobic selectivity, which confirms the successful preparation of CQDs with certain hydrophobicity.

Hydrophobic compounds such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and polar compounds such as sulfonamides were chosen as probes to evaluate the chromatographic performance of this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column in RPLC. To understand the effect of introducing 1,8-diaminooctane into CQDs on chromatographic performance, citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column was evaluated and used as a comparison. Moreover, commercial C18 column was also used as a comparison to study the hydrophobicity of the prepared amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column. To compare the separation performance of the three columns fairly, analysis time is stipulated as the common conditions. Fig. 6 shows the chromatograms of PAHs separated on amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column, citric acid CQDs column and commercial C18 column. C18 column shows well separation performance for PAHs with good peak shape. The amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column shows satisfactory separation performance but exists trailing phenomenon. The peak trailing may be due to the strong π-π stacking interaction between CQDs and aromatic compounds. Chromatographic parameters including k, selectivity factor, resolution, tailing factor and the number of theoretical plates (N) are listed in Table S1. All the tested PAHs are efficiently separated on the amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column, and the larger the conjugate system of the aromatic compound, the longer retention time is observed, which indicates the good aromatic selectivity of this CQDs/SiO2 column. The good aromatic selectivity roots in the highly delocalized conjugate system of π-electron in CQDs which can form π-π stacking interaction with aromatic compounds. Citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column have good aromatic selectivity for PAHs, but the leading peaks influence the resolution seriously. This may be due to that hydrophilic citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column is not proper for separating hydrophobic compounds under RPLC mode.

Fig. 7 shows the chromatograms of sulfonamides separated on amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column, citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column and commercial C18 column. Sulfonamides are not separated well on C18 column with leading peaks, this may be due to that hydrophobic C18 column is not suitable for separating strong polar compounds. The tested sulfonamides obtain good separation on citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column and achieve the highest separation efficiency on amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column. We also find that the retention orders of the tested sulfonamides are different on the three columns, which reflects the different retention mechanism. As we all know, C18 column depends on hydrophobic interaction to achieve separation. The retention order of sulfadiazine, sulfamerazine and sulfamethazine on amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column is in accord with that on C18 column, which indicates the good hydrophobic selectivity and hydrophobic interaction retention mechanism of amphipathic CQDs similar with octadecyl; while their retention order on citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column is opposite with that on C18 column, which indirectly indicates that introducing 1,8-diaminooctane containing long alkyl chain into CQDs indeed changes the hydrophily of CQDs and thus the retention mechanism of stationary phases. In addition to hydrophobic retention mechanism, this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column also shows electrostatic retention interaction which is different from C18 column. Sulfapyridine and sulfathiazole have stronger retention than sulfadiazine, this may be due to that pyridine and thiazole groups have weaker electrostatic repulsion interaction with amphipathic CQDs than pyrimidine group since there are many alkalic N-containing groups in amphipathic CQDs which can form quaternary amine and thus electrostatic repulsion interaction with sulfonamides.

Though alkyl chain is introduced into the CQDs, CQDs still have some hydrophilic groups including carboxyl, hydroxy and aminos. To verify the intrinsic hydrophilic property of the prepared CQDs, the chromatographic performance of this CQDs/SiO2 column was further investigated in HILIC mode. It has been widely known that the retention behaviour of HILIC is contrary to that of RPLC in some respects. In HILIC, the retention time (tR) of polar compounds increases with the increasing of organic solvent in mobile phases. Here, alkaloids were chosen as probes to explore the effect of ACN content on retention. Dependence of log k on volume fraction of ACN (10%–90%) is shown in Fig. 8 . It can be seen that the retention of alkaloids has two change rules with the variation of ACN content in mobile phases. First, log k values of alkaloids decrease with the ACN content increasing from 10% to 50%, and then increase with the ACN content continually increasing from 60% to 90%. This phenomenon demonstrates that this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase switches from RPLC to HILIC with the variation of organic solvent content in mobile phases. When ACN content exceeds 60% in mobile phase, the surface of CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase is occupied by a water-rich layer thus hydrophilic interaction emerges. The above experimental results indicate that this CQDs/SiO2 column has typical characteristics of RPLC/HILIC mixed-mode chromatography due to the amphiphilicity of the prepared CQDs.

Hydrophilic and polar compounds including nucleosides and bases, amino acids, β-adrenoceptor blockers and agonists, antibiotics and alkaloids were chosen as analytes to evaluate the separation performance of the prepared amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column in HILIC mode. Commercial NH2 column was used as a comparison to measure the hydrophily of this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column, and citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column was also evaluated to understand the effect of adding alkyl chain into CQDs. Fig. 9 shows the chromatograms of nucleosides and bases mixture separated on amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column, citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column and commercial NH2 column. The amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column shows better separation results than the other two columns. 14 nucleosides and bases commonly existing in living organisms achieve good separation on this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column with high column efficiency. According to the mobile phase conditions, we can deduce that the hydrophily of amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column is weaker than NH2 column and citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column since weaker eluent is used during the separation process on amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column.

Amino acids, the essential nutrients and basic building blocks of proteins, are also separated well on this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column (Fig. S4, S5), which represents the good hydrophilic performance of the synthesized CQDs and the potential applicability of this CQDs/SiO2 column in biological substances analysis. Medicines including β-adrenoceptor blockers and agonists, and some antibiotics are also separated well on this CQDs/SiO2 column (Fig. S6, S7), which indicates that this CQDs/SiO2 column is proper for pharmaceutical separation and analysis after further optimization. This amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column can also be applied for separating alkaloids (Fig. S8). From the compared chromatograms, it can be seen that some analytes which are not efficiently separated on commercial NH2 column and citric acid CQDs/SiO2 column obtain good separation on the amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column, which presents the synergistic effect of multifunctional groups including alkyl chains, amino, carboxy and hydroxy groups on the separation selectivity.

To deeply understand the retention mechanism of this novel CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase, LSER model which can help us obtain magnitude estimate of individual retention interaction was established in this work. The essential theory of LSER can be expressed by the following equation proposed by Chirita et al. [33] according to original Abraham equation.
(2) log k = eE + sS + aA + bB + vV + d −D- + d +D+ + c

In this equation, capital letters represent the solute descriptors, while lower case letters represent the system constants, related to the complementary effect of the stationary phase on corresponding interaction. K is retention factor. c is the model intercept term, is not assigned any chemical significance. E is the excess molar refraction and models polarizability contributions from n and π electrons. S is the solute dipolarity/polarizability. A and B are the overall hydrogen-bond acidity and basicity of solute. V is the McGowan characteristic volume. D− represents the negative charge carried by anionic and zwitterionic species, and D+ represents the positive charge carried by cationic and zwitterionic species.

To construct the LSER model, 25 analytes consisted of neutral, acidic, basic, and amphoteric molecules were analyzed under RPLC mode. The mobile phase condition was set at 30:70 MeOH/20 mM ammonium acetate aqueous solution (pH 7.0) to achieve appropriate retention time for all the tested solutes. Solute descriptor values were obtained from the published literatures [33–37] and charge related descriptors D+ and D− at pH 7.0 were calculated according to equations in Ref. [33]. All the solute descriptors and logk values were listed in Table S2. The system coefficients listed in Table 2 were obtained through multilinear regression of the retention data and the solute descriptors.

The magnitude of system constants (e, s, a, b, v, d − and d +) reflect the difference of that specific property between the mobile phase and stationary phase. If a particular system constant is numerically large, then any solute having the complementary property will interact very strongly with either the stationary phase (if the system constant is positive) or the mobile phase (if the system constant is negative). From the system constants listed in Table 2, we can observe that this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase has large and opposite b and v coefficients. Such large and opposite b and v system constants are always observed in RPLC systems [38,39]. The LSER analysis displays that this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase indeed possesses RPLC retention behaviour.

The v system constant represents the tendency of the chromatographic system to interact with solutes through hydrophobic interaction. A positive v value means a stronger interaction between solute and stationary phase than mobile phase. In this case, v value is positive and is the largest one compared with other system constants, which means hydrophobic interaction is the main retention interaction of this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase under RPLC mode. The hydrophobic property of this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase can be attributed to the aliphatic chain in the synthesized CQDs.

The e parameter reflects the tendency of the chromatographic system to interact with solutes through π-π interaction. A large and positive e value can be observed, signifying the high capability of π-π stacking interaction of this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase. This behaviour can be attributed to the highly delocalized conjugate system of π-electron in CQDs.

The s coefficient represents the tendency of the chromatographic system to interact with dipolar and/or polarizable solutes. In the present case, the system has a negative s value, indicating that dipole-dipole interaction is stronger between solute and mobile phase than stationary phase. This is reasonable since MeOH and water are much more polar than the stationary phase under RPLC condition.

The a and b system constants denote the tendency of chromatographic system to interact with solutes through hydrogen bonding interactions. The a coefficient is a measure of the hydrogen-bond basicity or hydrogen-bond acceptor property of the stationary phase, because acidic solutes (having large A values) will interact with a basic stationary phase. On the contrary, the b coefficient represents the hydrogen-bond acidity or hydrogen-bond donor property of the stationary phase, since basic solutes (having large B values) will interact with an acidic stationary phase. This CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase has a large and negative b value similar to conventional C18 stationary phase [38,39]. This CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase displays typical RPLC features but with peculiar character in the aspect of a parameter. A small but positive a value can be observed, which indicates this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase can form weak hydrogen-bonding interaction with acidic solutes. The hydrogen-bonding acceptance ability of this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase can be attributed to the oxygen-containing and nitrogenous polar groups in the CQDs. These coefficients could be confirmed by the experimental observations since the CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase showed stronger retention for acid compounds which could be seen as hydrogen-bonding donors.

The d system constant is a measure of the electrostatic interaction of the stationary phase. A positive d − and a negative d + coefficients can be observed in this system, which indicates the presence of electrostatic attraction interaction with negatively charged solutes and electrostatic repulsion interaction with positively charged solutes. The electrostatic interaction ability of this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase can be attributed to the alkaline N-containing groups in CQDs which can form quaternary amines in neutral conditions.

The reliability of LSER model for studying the retention interactions of this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase was justified by comparing the values of experimental log k and log k calculated according to the LSER formula. Plot of experimental log k verses calculated log k was shown in Fig. 10 . None of the solutes is outliers and the adjusted coefficient of determination (R2 adj) is 0.92, which indicates the data calculated from the LSER model are reliable.

In conclusion, this CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase could provide typical RPLC retention mechanism with hydrophobic interaction, π-π stacking, hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic interactions as main retention interactions. The conclusion drawn from the mathematic model agrees with the experimental results that this CQDs/SiO2 column presents good hydrophobic selectivity, aromatic selectivity and hydrogen-bonding property.

The stability is one of the essential parameters of the chromatography column. First, the stability of this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column was inspected by studying the tR of five model analytes under continuous operation with 60% MeOH/H2O as eluent over 80 h. The tR and N for all model analytes were basically unchanged, see Fig. S9. The single CQDs/SiO2 column has been used intermittently in our laboratory for 6 months, and there is no obvious loss of column performance.

Considering that silica gel is stable in the pH range of 2.0–8.0, the tolerance under acid and alkaline conditions of this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column was tested in pH 3.0 and 8.0, respectively. 90:10 ACN/20 mM ammonium formate (pH 3.0 and 8.0) was passed through the column for fixed periods and chromatography separation was carried out under the same condition and the process repeated. Both acid and base exposure experiments were carried out until 96 h passed. The separation results are shown in Fig. S10 and S11. There is no obvious loss of column performance, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of tR was below 0.69% (acid condition) and 0.89% (basic condition) for the tested compounds. Test results show this CQDs/SiO2 column is stable in the pH range of 3.0–8.0.

The stability of this amphipathic CQDs/SiO2 column under different temperatures was tested. Considering the volatilization of mobile phase, 40% ACN/H2O was passed through the column under 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C for 24 h, respectively. Compared the separation results before and after the temperature treatment (Fig. S12), there is no obvious decrease of column performance. Test results show this CQDs/SiO2 column meets the requirements of daily laboratory analysis.

The run-to-run and day-to-day repeatability, and column-to-column reproducibility of CQDs/SiO2 column were tested by the separation of nucleosides. The RSD values of k, peak area (A) and N are shown in Table S3. The RSD values of k were less than 0.46% (n = 10, run-to-run), 0.51% (n = 3, day-to-day) and 5.41% (n = 3, column-to-column), showing satisfactory repeatability and reproducibility.

Considering that the current applications of CQDs in HPLC are all in HILIC, a novel amphipathic CQDs with both hydrophobicity and hydrophily is designed and synthesized for RPLC/HILIC. The amphipathic CQDs are prepared by using 1,8-diaminooctane and citric acid as carbon sources. Characterization results indicate alkyl chain is successfully introduced into CQDs, which enable CQDs certain hydrophobicity and thus chromatography performance of RPLC. The prepared CQDs are further modified on silica forming CQDs/SiO2 stationary phase and applied in RPLC and HILIC. Several kinds of compounds including alkylbenzenes, PAHs, sulfonamides, nucleosides and bases, amino acids, β-adrenoceptor blockers and agonists, antibiotics and alkaloids obtained satisfactory separation on this CQDs/SiO2 column. Through investigating the relationship between retention time and the proportion of organic solvents in mobile phase, it is found that this CQDs/SiO2 column has typical characteristic of RPLC/HILIC. LSER model is established in order to get an in-depth understanding of the retention mechanism, it is concluded that hydrophobic interaction, π-π stacking, hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic interactions are main retention interactions under RPLC mode. This work provides a new approach for the design and synthesis of amphipathic CQDs, many kinds of amphipathic CQDs with different functional properties can be prepared using similar methods. We may find that amphipathic CQDs with both hydrophobicity and hydrophily are good multifunctional separation materials and have great prospect in mixed-mode chromatography.
The authors promise that this work is original, the experimental data is real and there is no academic misconduct. The same work is not submitted to other journals.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.
